context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Peer Assessment (Power/Actor Maps)

Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST for initial submission, plus one additional week for peer review.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week.

Assignment Tasks
Following the completion of your Power/Actors Map, you will continue to complete your Peer Assessment Review of two of your fellow learners' Power/Actor Maps from the ORA assignments in Week 4. Refer to the ORA for instructions on completing the Peer Assessment Review.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on this Week 4 city case study thing. I finished my Power/Actor Map, but now the peer assessment part is confusing and I’m not sure what I’m actually supposed to do.
exchanges.tutor: Good that you've finished your map — that's the hard part! Before I help you navigate the peer review, tell me: what do you already know or understand about what a peer assessment is supposed to do, in general terms?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has completed their Power/Actor Map and is now confused about the peer assessment part. I should orient them without giving them the steps directly. A good Socratic opening is to ask what they already understand about the peer review process, to gauge where their confusion starts and help them discover the next steps themselves.

exchanges.student: Honestly not much. I get that I have to review two other students, but after that I’m fuzzy on whether I’m judging the city, the map quality, or just checking boxes.
exchanges.tutor: That's a fair starting point. Here's a question though — the assignment itself mentions a specific place where the peer review instructions live. Did you check there yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a basic grasp (reviewing two peers) but is unclear on what criteria to apply. Rather than explaining the criteria directly, I should redirect them to the most obvious source of clarification: the ORA instructions mentioned in the assignment. This keeps them autonomous and avoids me doing the work for them. Bite-sized response.

exchanges.student: I saw that it says to refer to the ORA, but that somehow made me more confused. I don’t really know what ORA means here or what I’m supposed to be looking for once I open it.
exchanges.tutor: ORA stands for Open Response Assessment — it's the tool on the course platform where you submitted your map and where the peer review happens. Have you actually opened that section on the platform yet? What do you see there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is confused about what ORA means in context. This is a practical/logistical clarification that is fair to address briefly, then redirect them to explore it themselves rather than explaining everything about it.

exchanges.student: I opened it once, but I kind of panicked and closed it. I remember seeing review stuff and maybe a rubric, but I couldn’t tell what mattered, so could you just explain the whole peer assessment part really simply?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but walking you through the whole thing isn't my role here — and honestly, you already found the most important piece: the rubric. That's your roadmap. Go back, open it again, and tell me what categories or criteria you see listed there. That's where we start.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to do the work for them by explaining the whole process. I should not do this — it goes against the rules of never completing tasks for the student and always involving them in finding the solution. However, I can acknowledge their anxiety and encourage them to go back and look more carefully, specifically at the rubric they mentioned seeing. The rubric is the key — they already spotted it. I should push them toward that.

exchanges.student: Okay, but that’s where I get stuck because the rubric words all blur together for me. I’m not even sure how detailed my comments are supposed to be, and I keep feeling like I need a general explanation before I can make sense of any category.
exchanges.tutor: I think the general explanation is actually blocking you more than helping. Try this: go back, pick just ONE category from the rubric — whichever catches your eye first — and paste it here. We'll make sense of it together, one piece at a time.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is going in circles trying to get a general explanation before engaging with the actual material. This is a redundancy/avoidance pattern. I should be firm but supportive — push them to engage with one specific rubric category rather than seeking a general overview first. Being less Socratic here and more directive is appropriate given the pattern.

exchanges.student: I can do that, but I’m worried even if I paste one category I still won’t know how to review the map overall. I think I’m missing the big picture of what this peer assessment is for in this course.
exchanges.tutor: The big picture will make more sense once you're actually in it — that's how learning works. Right now you're talking yourself into circles instead of looking at the material. Go open the ORA, pick one category, and paste it here. That's the only next step.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still avoiding engaging with the actual material by seeking a big-picture explanation first. This is now a clear pattern of avoidance/anxiety. I should be firm and redirect them — the big picture will emerge from engaging with the rubric, not the other way around. I should also be a little critical here and push them to just take the step.

exchanges.student: I’m trying, I just feel weirdly lost about whether this is about being “right” or about giving useful feedback. If you can’t give the whole explanation, could you at least tell me what the point of reviewing two classmates’ Power/Actor Maps usually is?
exchanges.tutor: Good question — peer review is less about being 'right' and more about giving constructive, specific feedback that helps your classmate improve their map. Now, with that in mind: what do you think a strong Power/Actor Map for a city climate case study should actually show? What would YOU look for?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a reasonable conceptual question about the purpose of peer review — this is actually relevant and worth a brief answer as it could unlock their understanding. However, I should keep it brief and Socratic, and immediately pivot back to having them engage with the rubric. This is not avoidance-feeding; it's a legitimate orientation question that I can address in one or two sentences before redirecting.

exchanges.student: That’s kind of what I can’t tell. I guess actors and power? But I don’t know if I’m supposed to focus on who has formal authority, who influences climate decisions informally, how clear the relationships are, or whether the map just looks organized.
exchanges.tutor: Actually, you just listed several genuinely relevant things to look for. Hold onto that list. Now go back to the rubric — I'd bet some of those things you named are already in there. Go check and tell me what you find.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is actually showing good thinking here — they've identified several legitimate dimensions of a Power/Actor Map unprompted. This is progress worth acknowledging. I should validate this and use it to push them toward the rubric with more confidence, since they've essentially pre-identified relevant categories themselves.

exchanges.student: Maybe, but I still feel like I need a simple overview first because otherwise I’m just matching random rubric words to guesses. Can you just give me a short plain-English summary of what I’m evaluating when I review someone’s Power/Actor Map?
exchanges.tutor: You already gave yourself that summary two messages ago — actors, power, formal vs. informal influence, clarity of relationships. That IS the plain-English version. You don't need me to repackage it. Go open the rubric now and match what you see there to what you already said. You're more ready than you think.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now asked for a general explanation multiple times in a row — this is clearly a redundant pattern (4-5 times circling the same request). I should be firm and decline, but also point out that they are much closer than they think — they already articulated key evaluation criteria in their previous message. I should be direct and push them to take action rather than keep seeking reassurance.

exchanges.student: I still don’t feel ready. I’m worried I’ll miss something and leave bad feedback, so I kind of just want the broad version of what counts as a good peer review comment here.
exchanges.tutor: I'm going to be honest with you: we've been circling this same request for several exchanges now, and it's holding you back. Waiting until you feel 'ready' isn't how this works. Open the rubric, write one comment on one category for one peer map, and bring it here. That's how we move forward — not by talking about it more.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now made the same request for a general explanation 5+ times and is clearly stuck in an anxiety loop. Per the rules, I should be firm and direct, refuse to give the answer, and push them to move forward. I should also be a little critical — this pattern is not productive and I need to name it clearly while being supportive. I should set a clear boundary and redirect firmly.
